results.title: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Evaluating and Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use
results.content:
We introduce SkillCoach, a self-evolving rubric framework for evaluating and enhancing agentic skill-use. SkillCoach derives skill-grounded process rubrics from real rollouts and evaluates trajectories along four dimensions: skill selection, skill following, skill composition, and skill-grounded reflection. It keeps the external verifier as a separate outcome signal, allowing process quality to be distinguished from accidental task success. The evolved rubrics further serve as process supervision for selecting high-quality training trajectories.
Experiments show that evolved rubrics substantially improve evaluation quality, expose failures hidden by final accuracy, and provide stronger supervision signals than outcome-only filtering for enhancing agentic skill-use. [...] We propose SkillCoach, the first self-evolving rubric framework for evaluating and enhancing agentic skill-use. We define agentic skill-use as a trajectory-level meta-ability with four dimensions: skill selection, skill following, skill composition, and skill-grounded reflection. The external verifier is kept separate, which allows us to distinguish reliable skill-use behavior from accidental verifier passing. The evolved rubrics are then used in two ways: they diagnose skill-use failures, and they select high-quality trajectories for supervised skill-use training. [...] SkillCoach is designed for two deployment realities. First, we focus on skill-dependent tasks, where strong agents struggle without the relevant skill but improve when the gold skill is available. This shifts our focus from evolving skills to evolving rubrics that check how skills are used. Second, we evaluate agents in distractor-augmented skill libraries, where gold skills are mixed with distractor skills, approximating enterprise repositories with overlapping workflows.

results.title: SkillCoach: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use
results.content: Introducing the SkillCoach framework that helps giant language model (LLM) agents effectively utilize complex technology libraries. The existing method of simply judging the success of the final result has the limitation of not being able to capture the problem of the agent accidentally getting the right answer or going through the wrong process. To address this, SkillCoach creates a self-evolving rubric that evaluates the agent's course of conduct in four dimensions: skill selection, procedural compliance, skill combination, and result reflection. According to research, the system significantly improves the agent's proficiency by accurately diagnosing the agent's potential failure points and selecting high-quality learning data. As a result, this framework ensures that agents operate [...] in the correct sequential order. And finally, skill grounded reflection. This is effectively analyzing intermediate artifacts and outputs to adjust the trajectory mid-flight. These four pillars form the total meta ability matrix, and failing at literally any one of them compromises the entire agentic loop. Next up, section four, the self-evolving rubric pipeline. To calibrate autonomously, the pipeline extracts highly visible trajectory evidence. We're talking about distinct file edits, document reads, and specific parameter tool calls. It then proposes targeted arbitration patches based purely on this calibration data. And this highlights the systems absolute stability because it utilizes a strict hard gate. The evolution is kept at a maximum of six rounds and automatically halts if [...] As a result, this framework ensures that agents operate more reliably even in large environments, such as enterprise libraries.

results.title: Skills-Coach: An Automated Framework for Agent Skill Self-Evolution
results.content: -Coach is an innovative self-evolving framework developed to improve the functionality of large-scale language model (LLM)-based agents in an automated way. The system utilizes training-free GRPO algorithms to overcome the limitations of a fragmented technology ecosystem to optimize agents' command guidelines and code on their own. The framework consists of four core modules, from modules that generate advanced test cases, to four core modules that are responsible for optimization, execution comparison, and precision evaluation. In particular, it is designed to support both virtual and physical modes for flexible performance verification regardless of the environment. To verify this, the researchers built the Skill-X benchmark, which includes 48 different technologies, and the experiment [...] specific isolated problems. And because their design is so localized, they inherently struggle to cover the functional requirements of complex multi-step tasks. You end up with this landscape defined by abundant volume but totally fragmented integration. The whole goal of skills coach is to break out of this trap and achieve autonomous evolution. So here is the architecture and notice how this four module setup completely removes the human from the equation. It's a fully closed loop iterative process. You feed it an initial skill and the pipeline just takes over. It autonomously handles task generation, optimization, execution and finally evaluation. There is literally no human in the loop guiding the optimization trajectory here. Let's move to the execution phase and see how this builds [...] All right, let's bypass the usual preamble and get straight into the architecture. We're looking at the newly published skills coach framework today. If you've been building or working with LLM agents, you know we're constantly hitting the wall with hard-coded limitations, right? Well, this pipeline completely automates self-evolution. We're not talking about manual localized patching here. We are going to strictly break down the mechanics and the empirical data behind autonomous skill expansion. And the kicker, it does all of this without gradient updates and without a human developer in the loop. Okay, let's dive into this core research question. Up to now, the whole paradigm has basically relied on us, the developers, manually patching agent capabilities. But the skills coach team

results.title: Paper page - SkillCoach: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Evaluating and Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use
results.content:
Skills are becoming a reusable operational layer for LLM agents, encoding SOPs, domain rules, tool workflows, scripts, and validation routines. In realistic skill repositories, overlapping skills make reliable skill-use difficult. Final verifier success is too coarse for both evaluation and training, since an agent may pass through trial and error while selecting distractor skills, skipping required steps, composing workflows incorrectly or omitting final checks. We introduce SkillCoach, a self-evolving rubric framework for evaluating and enhancing agentic skill-use. SkillCoach derives skill-grounded process rubrics from real rollouts and evaluates trajectories along four dimensions: skill selection, skill following, skill composition, and skill-grounded reflection. # SkillCoach: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Evaluating and Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use

## Abstract

SkillCoach is a self-evolving rubric framework that evaluates and improves agentic skill-use by analyzing skill selection, following, composition, and reflection processes, providing better supervision than outcome-only metrics. [...] and skill-grounded reflection. It keeps the external verifier as a separate outcome signal, allowing process quality to be distinguished from accidental task success. The evolved rubrics further serve as process supervision for selecting high-quality training trajectories. Experiments show that evolved rubrics substantially improve evaluation quality, expose failures hidden by final accuracy, and provide stronger supervision signals than outcome-only filtering for enhancing agentic skill-use.
